summary.intent: Address code review feedback on PR #691 in fogodev/ars-ui by fixing controlled-state lag bugs in NavigationMenu and getting CI green with Codex approval.
summary.outcome: All four original review threads were replied to, resolved, and re-triggered via @codex review. A second round of Codex findings (three more controlled-state lag cases) were patched, tested locally, and pushed as a follow-up commit. CI was progressing toward green at session end.

repo: NavigationMenu has a controlled vs. uncontrolled open-state distinction; event handlers must use the rendered/effective open item (from controlled props) rather than internal State alone, or they misfire when controlled props keep content visible after an ignored request.
ValueChange effects must capture and report the requested next value so controlled opens report the requested key and closes report None even before parent state syncs.
SetItems removals must queue ValueChange(None) when clearing the active value, and must clear pending open intent and cancel both OpenDelay and CloseDelay whenever the registry is replaced.
SyncControlledValue must clear stale hover/content state and cancel both delay effects when controlled props drive a new value.
The repo uses @codex review comment triggers on the PR to re-run automated AI review after each push.

code.finding: Event handling logic for SelectLink and PointerLeave that was patched to consider rendered/effective open state under controlled props.
SetItems removal path where ValueChange(None) queuing and intent/delay cancellation were added.
SyncControlledValue handler patched to clear stale hover/content state and cancel delay effects.
CloseTimerFired and reconverge logic inspected as part of diagnosing controlled-lag event handling.

workflow: Resolving PR review threads via GitHub GraphQL mutation (resolveReviewThread) is required in addition to replying to comments.
Posting @codex review as a PR comment is the mechanism to re-trigger Codex automated review after each fix push.

CI pipeline runs multiple sequential jobs (fmt, snapshots, clippy, mutual exclusion, workspace check, unit tests, coverage, Dioxus feature flags, release verification); polling with sleep+gh pr checks is the only way to track progress.
cargo llvm-cov with --summary-only is used for quick local coverage checks scoped to a module prefix.
cargo +nightly fmt --all --check is a required CI gate; always run before pushing.

friction: CI polling required repeated sleep+gh pr checks invocations with 180-240s waits due to no webhook/notification mechanism available in the session.
Codex produced a second wave of review findings after the first push, requiring another full fix-test-commit-push-resolve cycle.

The controlled-state lag pattern recurred across multiple event handlers, suggesting the original fix was incomplete and the root rule (use rendered state, not internal State) wasn't applied consistently the first time.
GitHub checks run many jobs sequentially, making total CI turnaround time long and requiring multiple polling rounds.
open_items: CI was still running (coverage, Dioxus feature flags, release verification pending) at session end — final green status not confirmed.
Codex had not yet responded to the second @codex review trigger by session end — possible additional findings may require another fix round.
The pattern of using rendered/effective open state vs. internal State in event handlers should be audited across other components in ars-ui that follow a similar controlled/uncontrolled pattern.
